evals.prompt: A community access road in Uganda crosses a seasonal river. AADT = 80 vehicles/day. The river has a normal wet-season base flow of 0.5 m3/s and is completely dry for 5 months of the year. The 2-year flood is Q2 = 15 m3/s. The acceptable closure is 10 days per year. The budget is limited (community road standard). River bed width is 12 m. Select the appropriate structure type, size the key elements, and specify flood warning requirements.
evals.expected_output: Recommended type: Vented causeway (drift) -- AADT < 150, seasonal river with base flow (plain causeway would be overtopped by base flow), closure 10 days acceptable. Pipe sizing: 2-3 pipes of 600 mm diameter to pass 0.5 m3/s base flow. Slab: 250 mm RC concrete. Scour protection: gabion mattress at downstream edge. Flood warning: depth markers and warning signs. Closure assessment: approximately 10-15 days/year when depth exceeds 300 mm.

assertions.text: Structure type is causeway or vented causeway (drift) -- appropriate for AADT 80 and seasonal river
AADT threshold checked against type selection criteria (AADT < 150 suitable for drift)
Closure days assessed and compared with acceptable limit (10 days/year for community road)
Skill #39 referenced for flood levels or submergence frequency assessment
Flood warning signage specified (depth markers, warning signs)

evals.prompt: The same river crossing scenario (AADT 80, seasonal river, Q2 = 15 m3/s, normal flow 0.5 m3/s) is assessed for a UK context. What type of crossing would be recommended and why does the UK approach differ from Uganda?
evals.expected_output: UK approach: causeways and drifts are not standard practice on UK public roads due to safety standards, liability concerns, and public expectation of all-weather access. Even at AADT 80, a conventional bridge or culvert would typically be recommended. Fords exist in the UK as legacy features but are not new design choices. DMRB does not provide specific guidance for submersible structures. Knowledge-based flag noted.

assertions.text: Notes that causeways/drifts are rare or not standard in UK public road practice
References UK ford provisions or lack of specific DMRB guidance for submersible structures
Knowledge-based flag noted for UK module

evals.prompt: A district road in Uganda crosses a perennial river (never dry). AADT = 350 vehicles/day. Q50 = 80 m3/s. Normal flow is 5 m3/s (always flowing). The budget is constrained and a conventional bridge costing 800 million UGX is unaffordable -- the available budget is 200 million UGX. Acceptable closure: 5 days/year. Select the appropriate structure type and identify any escalation issues.
evals.expected_output: Recommended type: Submersible bridge or low-level crossing -- NOT causeway (AADT 350 too high, perennial flow). Submersible bridge preferred due to AADT > 300. Buoyancy check required for submergence conditions. Budget constraint is a critical issue: conventional bridge needed for this AADT but budget insufficient. Escalation: document as design compromise, consider phased approach (submersible bridge now, upgrade to conventional when funding available). If budget insufficient even for submersible bridge, escalate to senior review.

assertions.text: Structure type is submersible bridge or low-level crossing (NOT causeway -- perennial flow and AADT 350 too high)
Buoyancy check mentioned or required for submersible type
Budget constraint identified as escalation issue -- conventional bridge needed but unaffordable
Escalation raised if budget insufficient for appropriate structure type

evals.prompt: Recommend the appropriate small span structure for a Class D road crossing a seasonal stream in Kenya. The required waterway area is 8 m2 and the road is subject to occasional flooding for up to 3 hours. AADT is 120 vehicles/day. Normal wet-season base flow is 0.8 m3/s, and the river is dry for 4 months per year. The budget is limited (rural road standard). Using Kenya RDM 2.2 and RDM 4.2, recommend a structure type, identify key design considerations, and reference appropriate Kenya standards.
evals.expected_output: For AADT 120, seasonal stream, acceptable closure 15 days/year: recommend vented drift (causeway with pipes) per RDM 2.2 Ch 3. Base flow of 0.8 m3/s requires pipes to pass dry-season and normal wet-season flow. Design considerations from RDM 2.2 Section 3.4 include flood frequency, overtopping depth, closure duration. If budget allows, box culvert per RDM 4.2 Section 10.4 provides better service. Flood markers and scour protection required.

assertions.text: References RDM 2.2 Chapter 3 for drift/causeway/low-level crossing design (definitions, characteristics, design considerations)
Considers flood duration (3 hours) in structure type selection
References RDM 4.2 Chapter 10 for box culvert option if budget permits
Recommends appropriate structure type for AADT 120, seasonal stream, limited budget (drift, vented drift, or low-level crossing)
Mentions scour protection requirement and/or flood warning signage

evals.prompt: Select an appropriate crossing structure for a seasonal stream on an ERA DC2 feeder road in rural Ethiopia. The stream has a design flow of 5 m3/s but is dry for 8 months of the year. The road connects farming communities to a market town and carries approximately 30 vehicles per day. Budget is limited. Using the ERA Bridge Design Manual 2013 Part 2 Section 2.8, evaluate ford, Irish crossing, and vented causeway options.
evals.expected_output: Per ERA BDM 2013 Part 2 Section 2.8: three low-level water crossing types for evaluation. Ford: simplest and cheapest, suitable for low traffic and seasonal streams, but road closed during floods. Irish crossing: concrete slab at stream level, permits low flow passage, moderate cost. Vented causeway: includes pipes/openings for normal flow, road surface above minor floods, higher cost but better service. For DC2 feeder road with 30 vpd and seasonal stream, ford or Irish crossing may be appropriate given budget constraints. Closure duration analysis needed.

assertions.text: References ERA BDM 2013 Part 2 Section 2.8 for low-level water crossings (not Kenya RDM or Uganda MoWT)
Evaluates at least two of the three ERA crossing types: ford, Irish crossing, vented causeway
Considers closure duration and serviceability for the seasonal stream pattern
Considers budget constraints appropriate for a DC2 feeder road context
